item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: Author here, approving this change.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Whats one use of a Spanish to English dictionary that makes the user allergic to words? Well, as a potential means of reigning in that one SCP that is literally a being made of words (spanish and english words for that matter), in the event it gets a little too dangerous. As such, I am recommending the following edit:

Adding the following paragraph before the Experiment Logs:

Use of SCP-1772's effects as a potential containment measures for SCP-1516 has been proposed by researchers on both projects. Investigation into the efficacy of such measures, as well as potential side effects, is currently under investigation.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: A simple way of overcoming the infection problem while looking for inaccuracies in the book would be to take a page from the scribe schoo; use the illiterate. There are a handful of SCiPs that have removed the ability to recognize written languages, let those handicapped researchers be usefull by comparing SCP- 1172 to a mon-anomalous copy page by page. By looking at the text as pictures, rather than as information, the infection should be neutralized.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I feel the SCP-1772-1 takes away from what could have been an alright if a bit silly article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That character was actually the inspiration for this SCP.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Reminds me of "Making Money" by Terry Pratchett. That's a good thing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I missed Taglia's comment back in March, so thanks for re-iterating it. Errors have now been fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: there were three pages… dammit taggy ninja'ing me by tons of months

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You swspped D-4020 with D-3720.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really liked the concept, and there's a good level of exploration, but I'm less keen on the contagion and the structuring. It feels a little bit like the author thought it needed more danger, and it comes across as a tad artificial. I would have preferred it as a safe yet intriguing object.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How is this not more popular? The general reaction seems to be "meh" but it seems like it oughta be higher than single digits…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: at least black-box the -, after all, the purpose of a black-box is so you don't know what they're trying to hide

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It got off to a rough start.

Since I wrote those two bits I'll take a look at them later unless Drew does so first. That long sentence could use a footnote to shorten it. The intent for the - 1 part is that the flaws and effect are the same, but I'll reread it to see about a better wording.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Couple of nitpicks, since I'm just only now stumbled on this.

discovered to have flaws (termed SCP-1772-1)

secondary effect caused by flaws in the dictionary was identified and labeled as SCP-1772-1

Inconsistent designations.

In addition to the allergic reaction caused by SCP-1772-1 instances being more intense; with lethal symptoms usually appearing between 4 to 8 exposures; SCP-1772-1 has been found to be contagious.

This sentence is extremely hard to read.

The concept doesn't do enough for me to upvote, yet I'm surprised to see it so lowly rated. Usually not-horrible ideas with decent execution, like this one, are rated somewhere in +15 - +25 range.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Stylistic choice. Unless absolutely necessary, I always blackbox the names of people & companies in my articles. ESPECIALLY if it's a real-world company.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Honestly, I don't think you should black out the title, It's pretty obvious from the - in the middle that it's from Merriam-Webster

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Amnesiacs are people suffering from amnesia.
Amnestics are drugs that induce amnesia.

But this is a relatively new consensus and not without some dissent. Both uses have been/are accepted, subject to individual taste and judgement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Class C Amnestics" - "Class C Amnesiacs" if I'm correct.

Other than that, it's ok, beside one issue - a D-class joking and conversating with a Guard would likely get him reprimanded or something. At least in my opinion, but opinions may vary.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
In my opinion the method of transferring allergies is overly-complicated, the basic idea of somehow developing a bad reaction to a word is a very cool one, but having a dictionary that transfers it through reading translated words you didn't know seems pointlessly complex. I would suggest simplifying it to some other method of transfer.
To add a bit more excitement you could also change the reactions to be more diverse, possibly having something to do with the actual word, though I feel like that might take away from the elegant simplicity it currently has. Just my thoughts.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Firstly I am a new user so please feel free to take what I say with a pinch of salt.

I didn't feel I could vote this up or down because there were both sections I liked and disliked. I particularly liked the ellegance of the containment procedures and the description up until its third paragraph had me quite interested. I am also a fan of the resistance to being recorded to media.

Unfortunately the latter description of the SCP was for me a little one-dimensional in the use of typical allergic responses and I feel that this prevents a level of mysetry and depth to the SCP. I am also a little disenchanted with the infection aspect of the SCP and to me it feels like an attempt to make the item more dangerous.

If I may be so bold as to offer suggestions for change I would like to see the typical allergic reactions present as a number of unknown an apparently unrelated medical complications of varying criticality, possibily being a purely psycosomatic response to the word they have become allergic to. Some infromation could also be provided on treatment with placebo's or with medicine appropriate to the symptoms or disorders presenting on the allergic subject. Fatalities could present where identification of appropriate treatment or a particularly harsh response meant treatment cannot be delivered in time.

I appologise for the long post but my final recommendation would be to keep the experiment logs but remove the infection aspect of the SCP. I feel this would simplify the SCP and give it the rounded and discreet feel of some of the better SCPs I have read (although I admit my SCP knowledge is not extensive).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did know what it meant, but I had forgotten until reading this page.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still like it, although it is better now.

item.title: Expansion Posted
item.content:encoded:
With Drewbear's permission I've posted an expansion on his SCP to add more zest.

Vote canceled due to conflict of interest. QQ

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nil vote.
It's weird, but only a little bit weird and I move on without feeling anything for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Contrary to the downvotes, I didn't dislike this.
It's not really thrilling, but an SCP doesn't really have to be exhilarating to be good. It's well written and intriguing at least.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The concept's fun, but it just kind of putters around, existing.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's well-written, which makes it a shame that I found the concept boring enough that I needed to downvote. The article could have been interesting enough to overcome it, but it's a higher bar and it didn't get above it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Que?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: People who didn't pay attention in Spanish, unite!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thirded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Same.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I did not know what "huevo" in Spanish meant up until this article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Huh, yeah, that would work, methinks. it would be a bit complicated with Fresa, though… I liked the creativity that went into that one. It's always fun to pull double meanings and such from different languages. Glad they didn't go with the easy joke with the eggs, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The author did imply the translations, yes; I thought it would be a simple change to add “Word Read: ‘huevo’ [trans: ‘egg’]” or something, if only to clarify what “dentistry” has to do with “strawberry”. But if no one else was confused, don't bother. It wouldn't affect my vote either way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The word allergy concept is clever, but the article itself falls flat for me. No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked this one. Upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Sorry, was half-asleep. Sounded good to the hispanic side of my brain (j/h are pretty similarly pronounced by us Spanish speakers, as are b/v)… My mistake lol

Edit: or, more likely it was just the touchscreen keypad on my phone being a jackass. J &H are right beside eachother lol

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh, "juevo" isn't anything. "Huevo," with a silent H, means egg.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Uh, what Vincent said. The words on the cards are the translations of the words they read. And yes, apparently that particular dictionary does translate "fresa" as either "strawberry" or "drill (in dentistry)" in the real world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I upvoted. I enjoyed the concept, and those last two experiment logs really intrigued me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Fresa=Strawberry.
Juevo=Egg.
I thought the author did a good job explaining those translations

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: On the other end of the scale, I'm Australian, so I've never had any need to know any Spanish at all except for the occasional reference in American TV shows. Hence, I found the test logs a bit confusing and presumptuous, without there being anything to link “fresa” with “strawberry” or “huevo” with “egg” and so on. Could the test logs possibly state what the English for all of those Spanish words is, for the sake of us poor non-American people?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I live in Texas, and honestly, you'd be surprised at the number of people who treat not knowing a word of Spanish as a mark of pride. Some people are terrible.

That being said, this article needs some work before I can justify upvoting it. The idea of words having a strong impact on the universe directly, not just through people, is interesting, but it needs to have some more cool things in it.

Some examples of the style of experiments I would run/use if I was writing this article/researching this anomaly.

1: Take a subject who speaks fluent Portuguese and English and knows no spanish. They learn a word in Spanish that is identical in pronunciation and spelling to the word they already know in Portuguese. Is this treated as learning a new word or not?

2: Take a subject who speaks Spanish or English poorly. What would happen to somebody who thought 'Preguntas' meant 'pregnancy' and not 'question'? Do they count as learning?

3: Have a subject self-teach using basic a basic Spanish text and this dictionary. What happens when they then try to teach another subject Spanish? Is there carry-over, or does the infection stop with them.

As it stands, it doesn't do enough exploration of the idea.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Most Americans may only speak one language, but I think you'd have to work pretty hard to find one who doesn't know what "hola" or "huevo" mean.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I enjoyed it. It's not pretentious — it's an article that has a concept and plays with it a bit. Definitely a solid read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: SCP-241 is a book that gives you allergies. Granted it's different enough for both to coexist, not an allergy to words, but still, this one doesn't do anything remotely interesting in comparison.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This lacks an "ah ha"moment, and it also lacks in a feel of direction methinks. it reads like some kind of purpose is missing here.

Edit: maybe I'm the one missing something here…
